question:
Which of the following would replace -%- and -#- respectively in the following expression so that T -≤- P, P>F holds true?-
$M -<- S -\%- T -≥- E  =  D;- P -≥- S -#- L  =  C ->- F$
q:
Which of the following would replace -%- and -#- respectively in the following expression so that T -≤- P, P>F holds true?-
$M -<- S -\%- T -≥- E  =  D;- P -≥- S -#- L  =  C ->- F$
explanation: $Replacing -\%- with -≥- and -#- with ->- gives: T -≤- S -≤- P ⇒ T -≤- P (holds true) and P -≥- S ->- L = C ->- F ⇒ P ->- F (holds true)$.

Directions (610) -: Read the following information carefully and answer the questions given below.
In a certain code language: 
"Farmers protest continue union" is written as "ae me le ue". 
"Need complete by farmers" is written as "de le te ge". 
"Continue need good union" is written as "te ae ue ce". 
"Union get by chance" is written as "de ye we ae". 
-
Q6. What is the possible code for "good way complete"?
q:
Directions (610) -: Read the following information carefully and answer the questions given below.
In a certain code language: 
"Farmers protest continue union" is written as "ae me le ue". 
"Need complete by farmers" is written as "de le te ge". 
"Continue need good union" is written as "te ae ue ce". 
"Union get by chance" is written as "de ye we ae". 
-
Q6. What is the possible code for "good way complete"?
explanation:
Codes: 
$farmers = le$
union = ae
$continue = ue$
 protest = me
 need = te
$by = de$
complete = ge
$good = ce$
$get/chance = ye/we$
For 'good way complete': good=ce, complete=ge, way = new code (pe). Thus, 'pe ce ge' is possible$.

question:
Statements: P -≤- Q, S -≥- T -<- M -<- P
Conclusions: 
I. T -<- Q
II. Q ->- S
q:
Statements: P -≤- Q, S -≥- T -<- M -<- P
Conclusions: 
I. T -<- Q
II. Q ->- S

options.text: If only conclusion I is true.
If only conclusion II is true.
If either conclusion I or II is true.
If neither conclusion I nor II is true.
If both conclusions I and II are true.

explanation: I: T -<- M -<- P -≤- Q implies T -<- Q (True). II: Q is not definitely related to S due to conflicting signs (False).

q:

II. F -≥- O

options.text: If only conclusion I is true.
If only conclusion II is true.
If either conclusion I or II is true.
If neither conclusion I nor II is true.
If both conclusions I and II are true.

explanation: I: O ->- M -≤- K (conflicting signs, False). II: F -<- M -<- O implies F -<- O, so F -≥- O is False. Neither is true.

subject: Quantitative Aptitude
question: A can do a piece of work in eighteen days, while B and C together can do the same work in six days. If the ratio of efficiency B to A is $8:5$, then find in how many days C can complete the same work alone?
q: A can do a piece of work in eighteen days, while B and C together can do the same work in six days. If the ratio of efficiency B to A is 8:5, then find in how many days C can complete the same work alone?
explanation: $Let A's efficiency = 5 units/day ⇒ Total work = 90. B's efficiency = 8. B+C efficiency = \frac{90}{6} = 15. Thus C's efficiency = 7. Required days for C = \frac{90}{7} = 12_\frac{6}{7} days$.

question: Manoj -&- Nilu invested Rs. -‘P'- and Rs. 6000 respectively, while Manoj left the business after eight months. If Manoj got 50% less profit than Nilu at the end of one year, then find -‘P'-?
q: Manoj -&- Nilu invested Rs. -‘P'- and Rs. 6000 respectively, while Manoj left the business after eight months. If Manoj got 50% less profit than Nilu at the end of one year, then find -‘P'-?
explanation: $Profit ratio Manoj -: Nilu = 1:2. (P 8) ÷ (6000 12) = \frac{1}{2} ⇒ P = Rs 4500$.

question: $A is three years elder than B and after two years the ratio of age of A to that of B will be 5:4. Find the average of age of A and B after four years?- (in years) -?$
q: A is three years elder than B and after two years the ratio of age of A to that of B will be 5:4. Find the average of age of A and B after four years?- (in years) -?
explanation: $Let B = n years, A = n + 3. (n+2) ÷ (n+5) = \frac{4}{5} ⇒ n = 10. A = 13, B = 10. After 4 years, ages are 17 and 14. Average = 15.5 years$.

question: A train running at the speed of 180 kmph passes a pole in 36 second, in how much time the train can cross 1200 meters long another train, which running in opposite direction with the speed of 108 kmph?
q: A train running at the speed of 180 kmph passes a pole in 36 second, in how much time the train can cross 1200 meters long another train, which running in opposite direction with the speed of 108 kmph?
explanation: $Length \text{ of } train 1 = 180 \frac{5}{18} 36 = 1800m. Relative speed = 50 + 30 = 80 m/s. Required time = (1800 + 1200)/80 = 37.5 seconds$.
